content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


January 30, 1989


Julie Meier Wright
4778M La Villa Marina
Marina Del Rey, CA 90292

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-88-439

Dear Ms. Wright:

You have written requesting information regarding two issues
which arose out of elections in 1988. The first issue you raised
involved returning absentee voter applications to a campaign office rather than to the registrar of voters. This is an issue
over which the Commission has no jurisdiction. We recommend you
address your concern to the Secretary of State or your local
registrar of voters.

The second issue involved a slate mailer. The Commission
shares your concern over potential abuses which may occur with
slate mailers. In 1987, the Commission successfully sponsored
legislation regulating slate mailers. (See enclosed manual.)
That legislation became effective on January 1, 1988. We are
continuing to monitor slate mailing activities in order to
determine whether further legislation will be needed in the
future. We appreciate the information you have provided to us in
that regard.

If you have any questions, or if you would like further
information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

By: John G. McLean
Counsel, Legal Division

DMG:JGM:ld


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Julie Meier Wright
4778M La Villa Marina
Marina del Rey, California 90292

FPPC

Nov 17 8:37 AM '88

November 12, 1988

Mr. John Larson, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Larson:

I am not at all knowledgeable about campaign law and I do recognize how difficult it is to legislate ethics, but I feel compelled to state my feelings about the kind of political practices that I found particularly repulsive during this election cycle.

Two examples of unethical practices came to light in Santa Monica alone: First, the Santa Monica for Renters Rights (SMRR) group conducted an absent voter drive where they had absent voter applications returned to a campaign office rather than to the Registrar of Voters. The possibilities for fraud are clear: all they have to do is selectively forward applications based on demographic probabilities whether the voter would be for or against rent control. I am certainly hopeful that the FPPC can go after those responsible.

The second problem was a slate mailer, and I enclose a copy of the mailing (I apologize that it is marked up). This mailer is misleading and fraudulent, even though it might somehow meet the letter of the law. First, it purports to represent Republican candidates, when in fact the candidates listed for the school board, the college board, the rent board and the city council would never under any circumstances be supported by the Republican Party. Also, despite some disclaimers, they use the name "Victory '88" as the organization behind the mailing. "Victory '88" was used throughout the election by the Republican National Committee in their mailings -- clearly this was an intent to mislead. They also indicate that those candidates marked with an asterisk helped to finance the mailing; I understand secondhand that in at least one case this is not true. It's pretty clear that insurance companies supporting proposition 101 were the catalyst behind this mailer

Mr. John Larson
November 12, 1988
Page Two

-- just another example of the extremes the insurance companies will go to
in an attempt to protect their lucrative business in California.

Perhaps outlawing all slate mailers is one solution to this kind of unethical
mailing.

Because of the misleading nature of the advertising on so many of the issues,
I think it would also be good to require all campaign advertising to carry the
formal name of the proposition on file with the Secretary of State, if not
more descriptive information.

If there is anything that I, as a citizen, can do to tighten up the laws on
campaign advertising and direct mail, I will be delighted to do it. If you
have any summary information on regulations affecting this kind of
campaign effort, I would be interested in having it.

Thanks for listening.

Sincerely,

[signature]

cc: John Greenwood, Legislative Coordinator

January 30, 1989


Julie Meier Wright
4778M La Villa Marina
Marina Del Rey, CA 90292

                                                Re: Your Request for Advice
                                                    Our File No. I-88-439

Dear Ms. Wright:

     You have written requesting information regarding two issues
which arose out of elections in 1988. The first issue you raised
involved returning absentee voter applications to a campaign of-
fice rather than to the Registrar of Voters. This is an issue
over which the Commission has no jurisdiction. We recommend you
address your concern to the Secretary of State or your local
registrar of voters.

     The second issue involved a slate mailer. The Commission
shares your concern over potential abuses which may occur with
slate mailers. In 1987, the Commission successfully sponsored
legislation requiring slate mailers. (See enclosed manual.)
That legislation became effective on January 1, 1988. We are
continuing to monitor slate mailing activities in order to
determine whether further legislation will be needed in the
future. We appreciate the information you have provided to us in
that regard.

     If you have any questions, or if you would like further
information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

                                                Sincerely,

                                                Diane M. Griffiths
                                                General Counsel

                                        By: John G. McLean
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

DMG:JGM:ld


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 18, 1988


Julie Meier Wright
4778M La Villa Marina
Marina del Rey, CA 90292

Re: 88-439

Dear Ms. Wright:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on November 17, 1988 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact John McLean, an attorney in the
Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice. If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to information needed. If your request is for
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can.
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec.
18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

The second problem was a state mailer, and I enclose a copy of the mailing (I apologize that it is marked up). It's pretty clear that insurance companies supporting proposition 101 were the catalyst behind this mailer

Mr. John Larson
November 12, 1988

-- just another example of the extremes the insurance companies will go to
in an attempt to protect their lucrative business in California.

Perhaps outlawing all state mailers is one solution to this kind of unethical
mailing.

Because of the misleading nature of the advertising on so many of the issues,

If there is anything that I, as a citizen, can do to tighten up the laws on
campaign advertising and direct mail, I will be delighted to do it. If you
have any summary information on regulations affecting this kind of
campaign effort, I would be interested in having it.

Thanks for listening.

Sincerely,

[signature]

cc: John Greenwood, Legislative Coordinator

Vote for one safe workplace
Bernardissen beat

SECTION 1

ELECTION TOPSODY NOVEMBER 8
BERNADANS
ALTERNATIVE SANDY MORNING

CAR-15 SONS #CR 22

JACQUELINE LINE BANS
252 HIGH ST
SANTA MONICA CA 90403

YOU
VOTE
HERE

YES
ON
97

Join Senator Pete Wilson and other
Republican leaders in voting YES on
97, for a safer workplace and lower
Worker's Compensation Insurance
rates.

YES ON 80

Governor Deukmejian says that
Proposition 80 is necessary ''to build
additional prison facilities or the courts
may begin to let felons out.''

PRESIDENT RONALD REAGAN

It is important that all Republicans
vote on Nov. 8th for the Bush-Quayle
ticket, to continue to build on the
achievements of the Reagan-Bush
Administration!

[Two images at top showing "BARBARA USHING" and "DALE WILSON"]

YES ON 101

Proposition 101—the Prop. 13 of the auto insurance initiatives!

Proposition 101 is the only auto insurance initiative which dramatically reduces your premiums and provides for legal reforms which end trial lawyer promoted lawsuits and fraudulent insurance claims. Proposition 101 mandates a roll back of your auto insurance rates to October, 1987 and then cuts them an average of 1/3.

Proposition 101 puts an average of $750 per year back in the pockets of average California families.

Proposition 101 is endorsed by leading California conservatives including State Senators Marian Bergeson and John Seymour, and Assemblyman Ross Johnson.

VOTE YES ON PROPOSITION 101

YES ON 98 — Better Schools Without Tax Increases. Ensures that your tax dollars are spent efficiently. Makes schools accountable by requiring progress reports on classroom discipline, test scores, drop-out rates, and quality of instruction.

YES ON 99 — Don't Be Fooled By Tobacco Industry Propaganda. Join our Republican Sheriff Sherman Block, the L.A. Times, the L.A. Board of Supervisors, and the L.A. Chamber of Commerce in supporting YES ON 99—TO SAVE LIVES.

NO ON 95 — Feeds The Bureaucrats, Not The Homeless. Clogs courts with predicted 1,000 new cases daily, requiring 18 new judges. California Taxpayers Association and California Chamber of Commerce oppose Prop. 95's costly new government bureaucracy.

NOTICE TO VOTERS:
THIS DOCUMENT WAS PREPARED BY VICTORY '88 NOT AN OFFICIAL POLITICAL PARTY ORGANIZATION. Appearance in this mailer does not necessarily imply endorsement of others in this mailer. Appearance is paid for and authorized by each candidate and ballot measure which is designated by an*.

sections.conclusion: The first issue involved returning absentee voter applications to a campaign office rather than to the Registrar of Voters. This is an issue over which the Commission has no jurisdiction. We recommend you address your concern to the Secretary of State or your local registrar of voters. The second issue involved a slate mailer. The Commission shares your concern over potential abuses which may occur with slate mailers. In 1987, the Commission successfully sponsored legislation regulating slate mailers. (See enclosed manual.) That legislation became effective on January 1, 1988. We are continuing to monitor slate mailing activities in order to determine whether further legislation will be needed in the future.
sections.question_synthetic: Julie Meier Wright submitted complaints regarding two election-related practices: (1) absentee voter applications being returned to a campaign office rather than to the Registrar of Voters, and (2) a slate mailer that she believed was misleading and fraudulent in its representation of candidates and use of the 'Victory '88' organization name.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined jurisdiction over the absentee voter application issue and recommended addressing it to the Secretary of State or local registrar. Regarding slate mailers, the FPPC acknowledged the concerns and noted that 1987 legislation regulating slate mailers became effective January 1, 1988, with the Commission continuing to monitor such activities.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of multiple pieces: incoming complaint letter (Nov 12, 1988), FPPC acknowledgment letter (Nov 18, 1988), and formal FPPC response letter (Jan 30, 1989). Slate mailer sample document included. Classified as 'correspondence' due to multi-part nature; primary FPPC response extracted. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers but substantive advice provided in response letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Julie Meier Wright submitted complaints regarding two election-related practices: (1) absentee voter applications being returned to a campaign office rather than to the Registrar of Voters, and (2) a slate mailer that she believed was misleading and fraudulent in its representation of candidates and use of the 'Victory '88' organization name.

CONCLUSION: The first issue involved returning absentee voter applications to a campaign office rather than to the Registrar of Voters. This is an issue over which the Commission has no jurisdiction. We recommend you address your concern to the Secretary of State or your local registrar of voters. The second issue involved a slate mailer. The Commission shares your concern over potential abuses which may occur with slate mailers. In 1987, the Commission successfully sponsored legislation regulating slate mailers. (See enclosed manual.) That legislation became effective on January 1, 1988. We are continuing to monitor slate mailing activities in order to determine whether further legislation will be needed in the future.

embedding.summary: This file contains Julie Meier Wright's November 1988 complaint letter to the FPPC regarding two questionable election practices in Santa Monica, followed by the FPPC's acknowledgment letter and formal response declining jurisdiction over the absentee voter application issue while addressing her slate mailer concerns.
